Opinion
The Case for Small · Simple · Forgotten

Less Is,
More.

On Wranglers, forgotten Japanese runabouts, and why the best things left behind are worth finding again.
Category Opinions
Read Time 12 min
Published May 2026

The phrase gets thrown around constantly, especially in the automotive world. "Less is more." It sounds like something printed on a motivational poster inside a minimalist design studio — the kind of place where everything is white, angular, and has no cup holders. But live with it for a moment in the context of cars, and it starts to mean something real. Something that cuts through the noise of bigger, heavier, more expensive, more complicated, more everything — and asks a genuinely uncomfortable question: what exactly did we gain by adding all of this stuff?

This article is not a comprehensive survey of all the ways the phrase applies. That would take a small library. I want to focus on something specific: the small, three-door, open-top, fun Japanese runabout SUV market — specifically the ones built in the 1980s and '90s — and what they tell us about where we are now and where some of us would rather be. To get there, we need to start with the king.

The Wrangler: The Vehicle That Won Everything and Lost Something

The Jeep Wrangler is probably one of the most recognized vehicles on the planet — the VW Beetle being the only real argument otherwise. Its origin is the war. Its image was seeded across Europe, Africa, and Asia before most of its eventual buyers were born. It has appeared in more war films than most actors. Its fundamental shape has remained, recognizably and stubbornly itself, from the day the military first needed something that could go anywhere to the day someone in Beverly Hills paid $62,000 for the same basic idea wrapped in leather and ambient lighting.

And here is what is remarkable about that: for a long time, the Wrangler had virtually zero competition. Some manufacturers saw the market. None could capture the audience well enough to keep their products alive — at least not in the United States. The Land Rover Defender was too expensive. Others lacked the timing or the marketing. The Wrangler's exclusivity and absence of real rivals didn't just keep it alive — it gave it one of the most extraordinary resale value stories in automotive history. At one point you could buy a Wrangler, drive it, enjoy it, and recoup close to sixty percent of your investment. A vehicle you actually used, depreciating at a fraction of the normal rate. That is not a car purchase. That is a financial instrument that also happened to take you to the top of a mountain with the doors off.

More than that — and this is the part that matters — the Wrangler did something no amount of horsepower or technology has ever reliably replicated. It made you feel like a child again. Not metaphorically. Actually. Doors off, roof gone, wind everywhere, the engine just loud enough to remind you it exists, the road just rough enough to remind you that you are moving through it rather than being conveyed above it. You become transported back to when you were a child riding your bike, not a care in the world. Everything about your day — your commute, your inbox, your mortgage, all of it — simply evaporated. The simplest form of joy was always the grandest. That is what the Wrangler, at its best, could deliver.

But something shifted. Somewhere around 2006, Jeep started to make its move. The two-door became four. New trim levels multiplied. The Patriot and Compass arrived to fill the entry-level void, which freed the Wrangler to climb into a new market — one defined not just by joy, but by status. The thing that once cost ordinary people an accessible amount of money and gave them an extraordinary feeling became, gradually, something else. An object of aspiration. A status symbol. A luxury item wearing muddy clothing.

By 2020, Ford arrived with the Bronco and gave the Wrangler its first real competition in decades. Both are good vehicles. Both are genuinely capable. Both now start north of $40,000 for a base two-door with a back seat that has the comfort and practicality of a very expensive afterthought. The exclusivity the Wrangler once wore like a badge of honor is gone. They are everywhere. And paradoxically, in becoming everything, the Wrangler became slightly less itself.

Do not misread this. The Wrangler is a great vehicle and still does what it does brilliantly. But it has climbed too high and left its bedrock fans below. It became a slave to the market — and in bending its will to the masses, it opened a door. The underdogs walked through it.

The Alternatives: The Ones Nobody Bought and Everyone Should Have

While the Wrangler was building its empire, a handful of small Japanese and Korean manufacturers were doing something similar — just at a fraction of the price, in a fraction of the spotlight, for a fraction of the audience that would come to appreciate them only after most of them had already disappeared.

The Suzuki Samurai. The Suzuki Sidekick. The Daihatsu Rocky. The first-generation two-door RAV4 with the soft top and the borrowed time. None of them are fast. None of them are quiet. None of them will make you feel like you're piloting a spacecraft. But every single one of them will make you feel like you're driving — which is, if we're honest about it, exactly what we keep saying we want more of and keep buying less of.

They are analog vehicles. Not just any analog vehicles — they are Japanese and Korean analog vehicles built in an era when reliability meant everything to their brands in the American market. They are simple, honest, easy to maintain, and cheaper to own than the alternative they represent. They provide all the same emotions as the Wrangler: the freedom, the openness, the low-speed adventure, the feeling of escape. And they do it while being unique, genuinely rare, and obtainable by people who aren't leasing a status symbol.

Everyone has a Wrangler. Their once-coveted exclusivity, the very thing that made them special, has become their tipping point — thank Malcolm Gladwell for that framing, it fits perfectly here. The underdogs, meanwhile, have no such problem. They never sold enough to become ubiquitous. And now, that failure has become their greatest asset.

The Suzuki Samurai

Suzuki is no stranger to small, fun, Jeep-like machines. A company founded in 1947, they had been building compact open-top 4x4s for decades before they took aim at the American market. The Samurai arrived in the US in 1985, carrying a 1.3-liter engine, a manual gearbox, a part-time 4WD system, and the general philosophy of a vehicle that had been designed to go anywhere a reasonable person might want to take it and quite a few places they probably shouldn't.

It was loved — genuinely, enthusiastically loved — until Consumer Reports ran its infamous 1988 rollover story, claiming the Samurai had a higher-than-normal propensity to flip during evasive maneuvers. Suzuki disputed the findings vigorously. The courts, years later, sided largely with Suzuki. But the damage to American sales was done. The Samurai's short wheelbase and high center of gravity, characteristics it shared with nearly every other compact off-roader of the era including the Wrangler, became a narrative that proved impossible to shake.

What it actually was, away from the controversy, was something wonderful: light, nimble, immediately communicative, and genuinely capable off-road in a way that belied its modest dimensions. The lack of sound deadening meant you heard everything. The small engine meant you felt every horsepower. The short wheelbase meant you felt every corner, every rut, every change in surface. It wasn't refinement. It was feedback. And feedback, it turns out, is exactly what makes a vehicle genuinely fun to drive.

The Suzuki Sidekick & Geo Tracker

The Samurai's replacement was the Sidekick — a vehicle born from one of automotive history's more pragmatic handshakes. GM wanted into the small SUV game. Suzuki already had the product and the engineering. The two companies worked it out, and the result was sold simultaneously as the Suzuki Sidekick and the Geo Tracker through GM's entry-level Geo division. Two names, one honest machine.

That handshake had an address. The two companies built a factory together — CAMI Automotive, a fifty-fifty joint venture in Ingersoll, Ontario — and starting in 1989 the Sidekick and the Tracker came down the very same assembly line, in the same building, bolted together by the same hands. They were not similar vehicles. They were the same vehicle wearing two different grilles — badge engineering at its most honest: one machine, photographed twice. In the earliest days, before the Canadian plant was fully up to speed, a good number of “American” Trackers were quietly built in Japan and shipped over. Some of the most domestic-looking little 4x4s of the era were never domestic at all.

So why two names for one truck? Because the same goal was being chased from opposite directions. Suzuki wanted to sell a Suzuki — to plant its flag in America on the strength of its own engineering, through its own modest dealer network. GM wanted a small SUV it didn’t have and didn’t care to invent, so it did the math and rebadged Suzuki’s. The clever part was the marketing: GM created Geo, an entire sub-brand whose whole job was to sell Japanese-designed cars to Americans who weren’t sure they trusted Japanese-designed cars — a friendly, almost-domestic badge sitting in thousands of Chevrolet showrooms with a national ad budget behind it. Same machine. Two completely different ways into the driveway. Suzuki sold you the engineering. GM sold you the comfort of a familiar logo.

And the mechanical differences between the two? Look as hard as you like — there essentially aren’t any. Same 1.6-liter four. Same body-on-frame chassis. Same part-time, dual-range four-wheel-drive system that made it an honest light truck rather than a tall hatchback in hiking boots. Suzuki’s catalog stretched a little wider at the edges — a cheaper 1.3-liter base model at one end, a hotter 1.8-liter Sport at the other — while the Tracker mostly held the affordable middle GM wanted it to own. Past that, the distinctions were skin-deep: a grille, a badge, a trim name (Suzuki’s JLX, Geo’s LSi), a different cover on a different brochure. Whichever one you found, wearing whichever badge, the machine underneath was the same — and the machine underneath was quietly, durably excellent.

The Sidekick was larger than the Samurai, slightly more refined, and considerably more practical. It offered a proper backseat. It could be optioned with a hardtop or a soft-top, full doors or half-doors. Its 1.6-liter engine produced modest but entirely sufficient power for a vehicle that weighed barely over 2,000 pounds. It was not trying to be fast. It was trying to be fun and capable and simple, and in all three categories it delivered without complaint for years and years and years.

These are turn-key vehicles. You do not troubleshoot a Sidekick. You start it and drive it, and it goes where you point it, and when you're done it waits patiently for next time. That is a feature, not a limitation.

The Daihatsu Rocky

Daihatsu is not a name most people associate with anything, let alone fun off-road gems. Which is, itself, the entire story of the Rocky in America.

Daihatsu officially sold cars in the US from 1988 to 1992, and the Rocky — based on the F300 series — was one of only a couple of models they offered. It was sold primarily on the West Coast, a calculated geographic entry that never had time to expand before the brand exited entirely. The window was too short and the timing too unfortunate. David lost to Goliath.

But the Rocky itself was something special. Its 1.6-liter inline-four produced around 94 horsepower and 94 lb-ft of torque. Those are not large numbers. But the Rocky weighed under 3,000 pounds, ran a real body-on-frame construction, carried a proper part-time 4WD system with selectable modes, and was built with the Land Cruiser philosophy at micro machine scale: go anywhere, open top, honest, no frills, what you see is what you get. Owners praised it in those exact terms. One early review described it as having the soul of a Land Cruiser at a budget price in a compact package. That is either the best or worst description of a small truck depending on how you feel about Land Cruisers, but either way it says everything you need to know.

Because the Rocky was sold only briefly in limited markets, barely any exist in the United States today. Clean examples fetch between $5,000 and $10,000, with exceptional survivors climbing higher. For a vehicle this rare, this honest, and this capable in its intended environment, that still feels like a bargain.

The Original Toyota RAV4 — Two-Door, Open Top

Before the RAV4 became one of the world's best-selling crossovers — a reliable, refined, eminently sensible choice for millions of people who wanted a higher seating position and nothing particularly surprising — it was something else entirely.

The first-generation two-door soft-top RAV4, launched in 1994 and sold in the US from 1996, was built to blur the boundary between city and trail. It was compact, playful, and genuinely adventurous in a way the name "Recreational Active Vehicle with 4-Wheel Drive" suggested before everyone forgot what the acronym stood for. The removable roof panels, the short wheelbase, the eager 2.0-liter engine — all of it conspired to create a vehicle that felt, above everything else, light. Not just physically. Emotionally. You did not drive the early two-door RAV4 to impress anyone. You drove it because it made ordinary places feel new.

It did not try to be rugged. It simply was. And then it grew up, grew four doors, grew quieter, grew safer and more practical and more capable and more everything — and somewhere in that growth, the original's particular magic quietly departed. The world has several million sensible RAV4s. It has almost no early two-door soft-tops in running condition. The ones that survive are objects of genuine desire precisely because of that scarcity.

"They are your childhood bike that you used to jump off on your friend's lawn and leave there without a care in the world. Your old t-shirt that fits great. Your old inexpensive sunglasses that you can't seem to lose or break no matter how hard you try."

— On what these vehicles actually feel like

The Betty Crocker Effect: Why the Brain Needs Something to Do

Here is a detour that is not a detour at all. Stay with me.

After World War II, American home life was thriving. Families were back together, the economy was growing, and the food industry — energized by the same packaged-goods technology developed for military rations — started bringing genuine convenience into kitchens. You could now buy a cake mix that required nothing more than water. The powdered egg was already in the box. The flour was measured. The chemistry was done. Just add water, stir, bake. Couldn't be simpler. Couldn't be easier.

And nobody bought it.

General Mills, owners of the Betty Crocker brand, were baffled. They brought in a consumer psychologist named Ernest Dichter — the man credited with inventing the modern focus group — to figure out what was going wrong. What Dichter found has resonated through psychology and marketing ever since: the mix was too easy. Women — who in the postwar years had been sent back to the home after proving themselves fully capable in the workforce — needed to feel that they had contributed something. That they had made something. A mix that only required water left no room for craft, for pride, for the satisfaction of a job done well. It felt like cheating. It felt hollow.

The fix was disarmingly simple: Dichter advised General Mills to remove the powdered egg from the mix and require the baker to add a fresh one. One egg. That is all it took. Suddenly the baker was participating. Suddenly there was a skill, a contribution, a moment of ownership. Sales of Betty Crocker instant cake mix soared. The product became less convenient — and became far more desirable in the process.

Psychologists now call this the IKEA Effect: the more effort we invest in something, the more we value it. IKEA furniture you assembled yourself — even poorly — feels more like yours than furniture that arrived fully formed. A meal you cooked, however simple, satisfies differently than one that was delivered. A drive you actually felt, in a vehicle that actually asked something of you, stays with you longer than a commute you were passively conveyed through.

The brain does not want cruise control. It wants to be engaged. It wants to feel the road.

The Samurai, the Sidekick, the Rocky, the early RAV4 — they are the fresh egg. They ask something of their driver. And in asking, they give back more than any of their specifications suggest they should.

The Generation That Wants the Egg: Why the Young Are Choosing Less

Here is where the story gets genuinely interesting — and genuinely current.

The generation that grew up with everything at their fingertips, every song ever recorded available on a device that fits in a pocket, every movie ever made a tap away, every answer to every question instantaneously accessible — that generation is now buying vinyl records. In 2025, US vinyl sales crossed $1 billion for the first time since 1983. Not driven by nostalgic Baby Boomers who remembered the format from the first time around. Driven largely by people in their late teens and twenties who have never not had streaming music and chose, deliberately, to go looking for something else.

A 2026 survey found that 63% of 18-to-24-year-olds reported interest in exploring a more analog lifestyle. They are buying CD players and film cameras. They are choosing finite playlists over infinite algorithms. One retro tech company selling record players, cameras, and cassette players to Gen Z reported that what resonates most is the experience of actually unplugging — of having a physical object, a ritual, a beginning and an end. "There's just something so meaningful and so special about having that physical object," the founder said. "I don't think we humans are ready to get rid of it."

DVDs are experiencing a quiet resurgence. Film cameras — actual 35mm film cameras, requiring actual film, requiring actual patience, producing a finite number of photographs that cannot be instantly reviewed and immediately deleted — are selling in numbers that no one predicted. The paradox of choice — that modern phenomenon where having access to everything somehow produces less satisfaction than having access to something specific and finite — is being rejected, quietly and deliberately, by people who grew up with nothing but choice.

This is the Betty Crocker effect playing out across an entire generation in real time. They have been handed the just-add-water mix their entire lives. And they are asking for the egg back.

The Samurai. The Rocky. The early RAV4. The Sidekick. They have always had a following — the Samurai most loudly — but what is happening now is something more than niche appreciation. These vehicles carry the same frequency as the vinyl record. They are tactile, finite, demanding, and gloriously imperfect. They do not isolate you from the experience. They are the experience. Small, mostly manual, loud with things to tell you, low on horsepower and high on feedback. You feel every one of those modest ponies. You feel every turn, every rut, every shimmy, every squeak that has character rather than fault. They are not trying to hide what they are. They are exactly what they look like, which in an era of performance theater is a genuinely radical thing to be.

The Beautiful Irony of Failure

Here is the part that deserves to be said plainly: these vehicles are rare today specifically because they failed when they were new.

Poor sales meant no successor models. No next-generation Daihatsu Rocky arrived because Daihatsu left the US entirely. No evolution of the two-door soft-top RAV4 emerged because Toyota listened to the market and built something practical. The Samurai controversy cut short what might have been a longer American story. The Sidekick is largely forgotten outside the communities that never forgot it. They did not have the offspring or the second act that would have kept them common.

And in not having those things, they became something the Wrangler — for all its success and all its genuine greatness — can never quite be again: rare. One-off models in limited supply, hard to find in running condition, carrying the specific charm that only comes from knowing that what you have is genuinely uncommon. You stand out not because you spent more, but because you found something most people drove past without noticing. You are wearing tie-dye when everyone else is in black and white. People stop you. They want to know what it is, how you found it, what it's like. They are interested in you because your vehicle has a personality, and personalities — real ones, not curated ones — are rare.

The Wrangler's success was its evolution. The underdogs' obscurity became their preservation. Less is more.

Suzuki Samurai

The defiant one. Shockingly capable, unapologetically small. The rollover controversy almost ended it but only made the faithful love it harder. Find one. Drive one. Understand.

Suzuki Sidekick / Geo Tracker

The pragmatic partnership that worked. Two names, one honest machine. Lightweight, simple, endlessly willing. The kind of vehicle that starts on the first try in 2026 and doesn't apologize for anything.

Daihatsu Rocky

America barely knew it existed. David genuinely lost to Goliath here. But find a clean one today and you have a Land Cruiser soul in a micro machine body. Almost impossible to find. Completely worth the search.

Toyota RAV4 — 2-Door Soft-Top

Before it grew up and became sensible, it was playful and light and genuinely adventurous. The world has millions of modern RAV4s. It has almost none of these. The ones that survive are worth every bit of what they cost.

Find More in Simplicity

There was a time — not long ago, but far enough to feel distant — when freedom on four wheels did not require a touchscreen the size of a laptop, a badge engineered to signal status, or forty-two thousand dollars for a two-door with a back seat no adult has ever been comfortable in. Freedom came in smaller forms. Lighter ones. Honest ones.

These vehicles are not perfect. They are not fast. They are not refined. But they are, in a way that matters deeply right now, authentic. They remind us that capability does not require excess. That enjoyment does not scale with horsepower. That the most memorable drives are rarely the fastest ones — they are the ones where you felt something, noticed something, arrived somewhere and couldn't quite explain why the journey was so good.

Less weight. Less complexity. Less pretense. More connection. More character. More of the thing you actually got into a car to find in the first place.

The Wrangler had to evolve. It had to become everything it once stood apart from. The market demanded it and the market got it. But the underdogs — the Rocky, the Samurai, the Sidekick, the little open-top RAV4 — never had to grow up. They stayed exactly what they were. Impractical by modern standards. Imperfect by any standard. And now, because of that, more desired than they ever were when new.

Less really is more. It just sometimes takes twenty years to notice.

The Carveyance Takeaway
The Best Alternatives to a $42,000 Wrangler Are Analog, Japanese, and Already Out There Waiting for You

If what you want is the feeling — the openness, the freedom, the analog joy, the sense that you are actually driving and not just being transported — then the Wrangler is not your only option. It may not even be your best one. The Samurai, the Rocky, the Sidekick, the early two-door RAV4 — they are simpler, cheaper, rarer, and in the ways that matter most, they are more. Find one in decent condition, maintain it honestly, and drive it with the doors off on a Saturday morning. You will understand immediately. And you will look back at the modern alternatives and think: why did I wait this long to ask for the egg?
